nativeRange.habitat: Endemic to the Philippines (Cebu and Luzon), where it forms large colonies in shallow, fast-flowing freshwater rivers in lowland forests, rooting via stolons in sandy to gravelly riverbeds; the only Schismatoglottis species known to grow as a fully aquatic plant

decorPreferences: loose sandy or fine-gravel substrate that the stolons can run through
nutrient-rich aqua soil or inert substrate supplemented with a root tab
moderate flow to mimic the species' fast-flowing river habitat
may also anchor to porous hardscape (lava rock, driftwood) but does not attach with adhesive roots like Bucephalandra or Anubias
stable, established tank — acclimate slowly to avoid melt

goodWith: shrimp (Neocaridina and Caridina) which graze biofilm without harming the plant
other low-light foreground/midground species such as Cryptocoryne parva, Bucephalandra and Anubias nana
avoidWith: large herbivorous fish (goldfish, silver dollars, large plecos)
vigorous diggers (large cichlids, geophagines) that will uproot the runners
newly set up tanks with unstable parameters

imageCandidates.notes: Product page features cultivated submerged specimen showing characteristic almond-shaped green leaves and dense substrate-rooted stand.
Product page with multiple submerged images showing growth habit and leaf morphology.

summary: Schismatoglottis prietoi is a small, fully aquatic aroid endemic to lowland rivers of Cebu and Luzon in the Philippines — the only fully aquatic member of an otherwise terrestrial genus closely allied to Bucephalandra. Unlike its Bornean cousins, it is a substrate-rooted foreground plant that spreads by stolons rather than an epiphyte.
careNotes: Schismatoglottis prietoi (Araceae) was first described in 2015 by Boyce, Medecilo and Wong from fast-flowing lowland rivers on Cebu (and later Luzon) in the Philippines, where it forms broad colonies anchored by stolons in sandy to gravelly riverbeds. It is taxonomically a sister to Bucephalandra within tribe Schismatoglottideae but, unlike Bucephalandra and the rest of the genus Schismatoglottis (which are mostly terrestrial rheophytes), S. prietoi is the only species in its genus known to grow fully submerged in nature. This has direct husbandry implications: it is treated as a substrate-rooted foreground/midground plant, not as an epiphyte. The creeping rhizome and runners are planted into a loose sandy or fine-gravel substrate (or nutrient-rich aqua soil) and the plant spreads laterally by stolons producing daughter plantlets, eventually forming a dense, broad stand somewhat reminiscent of small Cryptocoryne. Reported parameters across hobby sources cluster around 20-28 °C and pH 6.0-8.0; gH/kH are not restrictive, with sources describing it as tolerant from soft to moderately hard water (Aquasabi notes no special hardness demands; Aquarium Plants Factory tolerates soft-to-hard). Adult size in submerged form is small: leaves are typically 3-8 cm long and 1-2 cm wide on plants 2-8 cm tall in the wild, with cultivated specimens reaching the upper end of that range. Lighting is flexible — low light is sufficient for survival but medium light produces fuller, denser stands and the best leaf color; high light without CO2 will encourage algae on the slow-growing leaves. CO2 is not required but accelerates establishment and growth. Initial growth is slow while the plant transitions from emersed nursery form, then noticeably faster than Anubias or Bucephalandra once the runners take hold; aquarists who use root tabs in inert substrates report visibly better results. As with related aroids, sudden changes in temperature, pH or TDS during acclimation can trigger a melt of the original emersed leaves; introduce only into stable, established tanks and expect new submersed leaves to push from the rhizome over the following weeks. The plant can also be wedged into the crevices of porous lava rock or driftwood and will anchor with its roots, but it does not attach by adhesive roots the way Bucephalandra and Anubias do, so substrate planting is the more reliable approach.
breedingNotes: Standard hobby propagation is vegetative. The plant produces lateral runners (stolons) that develop daughter plantlets along the substrate; once a young plantlet has its own roots and 2-3 leaves it can be separated from the parent runner with sharp scissors and replanted. Mature rhizomes can also be divided in the same way as Bucephalandra, cutting at a natural bend with several leaves and visible roots per division. Sexual reproduction via the aroid spathe/spadix is not a practical hobby method.

primary.name: Aquasabi
primary.notes: Primary source. Aquasabi profile provides temperature (20-28 °C), pH (6-8), low-to-medium lighting, CO2 not required, slow-then-faster growth, foreground-to-midground placement, substrate-rooted with stolons (anchors in porous rock but not adhesive epiphyte), recommended 5-20 cm aquarium height, easy care, and Cebu Philippines origin.

additional.name: Wikipedia (Schismatoglottis prietoi)
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Araceae, Alismatales), 2015 description by Boyce, Medecilo & Wong, endemism to Cebu and Luzon (Philippines), fully aquatic habit in fast-flowing lowland forest rivers, leaf size 3-4 cm long by 1-2 cm wide with sharply pointed wavy-edged tips, plant height 2-8 cm, colonial growth by stolons, and that it is the only fully aquatic species in the genus.
